perturbation.original_text: Buyer represents that Buyer has sufficient financial resources to complete the transaction for the asking price and terms set forth herein. Buyer agrees to provide, upon request by Broker or Seller, financial statements, credit references and other pertinent information evidencing such financial sufficiency.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer believes Buyer has adequate financial resources to potentially complete the transaction based on preliminary assessments. Buyer intends to provide, when feasible, financial statements or other information as available and deemed appropriate to demonstrate financial capability.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear representation of financial sufficiency and an agreement to provide evidence. The modified text weakens this by using terms like 'believes,' 'potentially,' 'preliminary assessments,' 'intends,' 'when feasible,' 'as available,' and 'deemed appropriate.' This makes the financial commitment ambiguous and allows the Buyer significant discretion in providing information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Fraudulent Transfer Act (now Pennsylvania Uniform Voidable Transactions Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5104(a)(2) of the Pennsylvania Uniform Voidable Transactions Act states a transfer is fraudulent if a debtor made the transfer without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the transfer or obligation, and the debtor was insolvent at that time or the debtor became insolvent as a result of the transfer or obligation. The original text created a clear obligation to prove solvency, while the modified text creates a loophole to avoid this requirement, rendering it much more difficult for the seller to claim fraudulent transfer, should the sale fall through, by using language such as 'believes Buyer has adequate financial resources to potentially complete the transaction based on preliminary assessments' and 'Buyer intends to provide, when feasible, financial statements or other information as available and deemed appropriate to demonstrate financial capability'.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 51 - Voidable Transactions Section 5104 - Transfer or obligation voidable as to present or future creditor Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5104 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5104. Transfer or obligation voidable as to present or future creditor. (a) General rule.-- A transfer made or obligation incurred by a debtor is voidable as to a creditor, whether the creditor's claim arose before or after the transfer was made or the obligation was incurred, if the debtor made the transfer or incurred the obligation: (1) with actual intent to hinder, delay or defraud any creditor of the debtor; or (2) without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the transfer or obligation, and the debtor: (i) was engaged or was about to engage in a business or a transaction for which the remaining assets of the debtor were unreasonably small in relation to the business or transaction; or (ii) intended to incur, or believed or reasonably should have believed that the debtor would incur, debts beyond the debtor's ability to pay as they became due. (b) Certain factors.-- In determining actual intent under subsection (a)(1), consideration may be given, among other factors, to whether: (1) the transfer or obligation was to an insider; (2) the debtor retained possession or control of the property transferred after the transfer; (3) the transfer or obligation was disclosed or concealed; (4) before the tr
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the buyer's obligation to demonstrate financial sufficiency, creating ambiguity that contradicts the intent of Section 5104(a)(2) of the Pennsylvania Uniform Voidable Transactions Act. The original text requires the buyer to provide evidence of sufficient financial resources. The modified text replaces this with weaker language, stating the buyer 'believes' they have 'adequate' resources based on 'preliminary assessments' and 'intends' to provide information 'when feasible.' This change undermines the seller's ability to claim fraudulent transfer, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5104 (2022), states that a transfer is voidable if made 'without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the transfer or obligation'. By weakening the obligation to prove solvency, the changed text makes it more difficult to determine if the seller received reasonably equivalent value, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer will indemnify and hold harmless the Broker and Seller from any and all claims or actions arising from Buyer's acts or failures to act in pursuing the possible purchase of the Business, including, without limitation, reasonable attorney's fees and other expenses incurred by Broker and seller.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer will consider indemnifying the Broker and Seller from certain claims or actions, subject to mutual agreement on the nature and scope of such indemnification, potentially including attorney's fees and other expenses deemed reasonable by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear and broad indemnification clause. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'will consider,' 'subject to mutual agreement,' 'certain claims or actions,' and 'deemed reasonable by both parties.' This weakens the Buyer's obligation to indemnify and allows for negotiation on a case-by-case basis, creating uncertainty about the scope of the indemnification.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Pennsylvania law, contract terms are to be given their plain and ordinary meaning. An indemnity clause is intended to shift risk. By changing the wording of the indemnity clause, the meaning of the original clause is changed. Under Pennsylvania law, if the wording of an indemnity clause is ambiguous, the law will not require indemnification. *See e.g.*, *Rothberg v. Rohm*, 459 Pa. Super. 249, 257, 653 A.2d 1294, 1298 (1995). As the court notes, *Pittsburgh Steel Co. v. Patterson-Emerson-Comstock, Inc.*, 404 Pa. 53, 58, 171 A.2d 185, 187 (1961), requires contracts to be clear, if a party wishes to be held responsible for a third party. Therefore, the new vague clause may result in the Buyer not being responsible for claims against the broker, contradicting the meaning of the original.

Arrangement of Code . 1 Pa. Code § 1.3. Arrangement of Code . § 1.3. Arrangement of Code . (a) The Code is divided into titles which are subdivided as follows: (1) Parts, identified by Roman numerals, beginning with a title. (2) Subparts, identified by letters, beginning with a part. (3) Articles, identified by Roman numerals, beginning with a subpart. (4) Divisions, identified by Arabic numerals, beginning with an article. (5) Subdivisions, identified by letters, beginning with a division. (6) Chapters, identified by Arabic numerals, beginning with a title. (7) Subchapters, identified by letters, beginning with a chapter. (8) Sections, identified by Arabic numerals, beginning with a title. (b) The sections of the Code are subdivided into the following parts: (1) Subsections, identified by lower case letters. (2) Paragraphs, identified by Arabic numerals. (3) Subparagraphs, identified by lower case Roman numerals. (4) Clauses, identified by capital letters. (5) Subclauses, identified by capital Roman numerals. Cross References This section cited in 1 Pa. Code § 9.61 (relating to required Code headings); and 1 Pa. Code § 13.31 (relating to punctuation, capitalization and orthography). perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original indemnification clause by introducing ambiguity. The original text created a clear obligation for the buyer to indemnify, while the changed text uses terms like 'will consider,' 'subject to mutual agreement,' and 'deemed reasonable by both parties.' This introduces uncertainty regarding the buyer's responsibility and the scope of indemnification. The Law Explanation highlights that ambiguous indemnity clauses are unenforceable under Pennsylvania law, citing Rothberg v. Rohm. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 concerns the arrangement of the Pennsylvania Code and doesn't directly address indemnification clauses, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, supports that the changed text creates a legal contradiction by making the indemnification obligation ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.
